ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for macadamia milk specifically; A0FKP (nut-based milk substitute) is the closest defensible analogous code in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for macadamia milk as of 2026; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of unsweetened macadamia milk is one cup (approximately 240 ml), providing around 53 kcal, 0.96 g protein, 4.8 g fat, and 0.96 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Macadamia milk, unsweetened — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for unsweetened macadamia milk (cr_id F01020009). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from macadamia nut fatty acid profile per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 12131) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycaemic index of plant-based milk alternatives — analogous estimate for negligible-carbohydrate beverage
gi_source.source_match_notes: Unsweetened macadamia milk contains only 0.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g (0.96 g per 240 ml serving), which is insufficient to elicit a meaningful glycaemic response. No direct GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for unsweetened macadamia milk. By convention, beverages with negligible fermentable carbohydrate are assigned a nominal GI of 1 (effectively zero glycaemic impact) and classified as 'low' GI. Glycemic load per serving = (1 × 0.96) / 100 = 0.01, rounded to 0.0. This is consistent with the treatment of other negligible-carbohydrate plant milks in the GI literature.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (69 mg/100 g), no added sugar, MUFA-dominant fat profile
hypertension.guidance: Unsweetened macadamia milk is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium content of 69 mg/100 g is low, it contains no added sugars, and the predominant fat is oleic acid (MUFA), which supports endothelial function — however, individuals should verify the product label to confirm no added sodium or sweeteners in commercial variants.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.4 g/100 g), negligible sugar (0.2 g/100 g), MUFA-dominant fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened macadamia milk is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its negligible carbohydrate load (0.4 g/100 g) and very low sugar content (0.2 g/100 g), which will not meaningfully raise postprandial blood glucose; the MUFA-dominant fat profile (oleic acid) may further support insulin sensitivity per ADA dietary guidelines, but individuals should confirm the product is genuinely unsweetened.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High MUFA content (oleic acid), low saturated fat (~0.3 g/100 g), no cholesterol, no trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsweetened macadamia milk presents a favourable cardiovascular lipid profile — it is rich in monounsaturated fatty acids (oleic acid), contains minimal saturated fat (~0.3 g/100 g), is free of cholesterol and trans fats, and has no added sugars, making it a heart-friendly dairy alternative consistent with AHA dietary recommendations for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory profile from macadamia nuts; absence of saturated and trans fats
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Unsweetened macadamia milk is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the oleic acid (MUFA) content has documented anti-inflammatory properties, saturated fat is minimal (~0.3 g/100 g), and trans fat is absent — individuals should choose fortified variants to address potential calcium deficiency relevant to bone health in RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unsweetened macadamia milk is a dairy-free, plant-based milk alternative produced commercially by blending raw or roasted macadamia nuts with filtered water, then straining the mixture through a fine mesh or nut-milk bag to yield a smooth, pale-ivory liquid. The resulting beverage has a mild, subtly buttery and gently nutty flavour with a clean finish and a thinner, more watery consistency than full-fat cow's milk or canned coconut milk, though slightly creamier than rice milk. No sweeteners, flavourings, or thickeners are added in the unsweetened variant. Macadamia nuts (Macadamia integrifolia and M. tetraphylla) originate from subtropical rainforests in Queensland, Australia, and are now cultivated commercially in Australia, Hawaii, South Africa, and Kenya. The milk is used globally as a beverage, coffee creamer substitute, smoothie base, cereal topping, and cooking ingredient in low-carbohydrate and ketogenic recipes. Per 100 g, unsweetened macadamia milk provides approximately 22 kcal, 0.4 g protein, 2.0 g total fat (of which ~1.5 g is monounsaturated oleic acid, ~0.3 g saturated, ~0.04 g polyunsaturated), 0.4 g carbohydrate, 0.2 g sugar, 0.0 g dietary fibre, and 69 mg sodium. Calcium and iron are negligible (0.0 mg/100 g) in unfortified products; consumers should check labels for calcium-fortified variants. The fat profile is dominated by monounsaturated fatty acids (MUFA), particularly oleic acid, which is associated with reduced LDL cholesterol, improved endothelial function, and anti-inflammatory effects. Saturated fat is very low (~0.3 g/100 g), trans fat is absent, and cholesterol is zero as this is a plant-derived product. Clinically, unsweetened macadamia milk is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (negligible carbohydrate load, no postprandial glucose spike), hypertension (low sodium, no added sugar, MUFA-supportive endothelial function), cardiovascular disease (favourable lipid profile, no cholesterol), and rheumatoid arthritis (anti-inflammatory MUFA content). It is naturally lactose-free, gluten-free, vegan, and suitable for most halal dietary requirements. Glycemic index is effectively negligible (nominal GI 1, low category) due to the minimal carbohydrate content of 0.4 g/100 g.

meal_context: cooking and baking substitute for dairy milk

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01020009, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy macadamia nut profile (NDB 12131) and documented. All values are internally consistent and plausible for an unsweetened macadamia milk product.
notes: 1. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g/100 g as reported by HPB; this is confirmed zero for this processed liquid product — the straining process removes most fibre from the macadamia solids. 2. Calcium is 0.0 mg/100 g and iron is 0.0 mg/100 g as reported by HPB for this unfortified product; these are confirmed zero per the HPB source and reflect an unfortified commercial variant. Consumers should check product labels as fortified variants exist. 3. Saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.5 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.04 g/100 g) are estimated from the macadamia nut fatty acid profile per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 12131), scaled proportionally to the 2.0 g total fat per 100 g reported by HPB; HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown for this entry. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero; macadamia nuts do not naturally contain trans fats and no hydrogenated oils are used in unsweetened macadamia milk. 5. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100 g — naturally zero as this is a plant-based product containing no animal-derived ingredients. 6. potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for unsweetened macadamia milk are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific diluted product formulation as of 2026. 7. Glycemic index is set to 1 (nominal low GI) because unsweetened macadamia milk contains only 0.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, which is insufficient to elicit a meaningful glycaemic response; no direct GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this product; by convention, negligible-carbohydrate beverages are assigned a nominal GI of 1 and classified as low GI; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 8. FoodEx2 code A0FKP is an analogous estimate for nut-based milk substitute; no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists specifically for macadamia milk. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315695 (macadamia nut milk) is used as the primary ontology anchor. 10. This record reflects an unfortified, unsweetened macadamia milk; nutritional values may differ for fortified or flavoured commercial variants.
